coredata.dc:title: Improved corrosion resistance and surface stability of Mg–Ca alloys in simulated body fluid by quench-produced diamond coatings

coredata.dc:description: Magnesium alloys are attractive biodegradable materials for biomedical implants; however, their clinical application is severely limited by rapid corrosion in physiological environments. In this study, Quench-produced Diamond (Q-Dia) films were deposited on Mg–Ca alloy substrates at room temperature using the coaxial arc plasma deposition technique. Two surface pretreatments were employed prior to coating: (i) mechanical polishing and (ii) in-situ argon ion (Ar+) etching, to clarify their influence on film formation and corrosion behavior. The surface morphology and bonding structure of the Q-Dia films were characterized by scanning electron microscopy, atomic force microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and Raman spectroscopy, revealing dense diamond-like grain growth with mixed sp2/sp3 carbon bonding and a pronounced D-band feature. Corrosion performance was evaluated in simulated body fluid (SBF) at 37 °C using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization (PDP), with bare Mg–Ca alloy as a reference. The Q-Dia coating significantly enhanced corrosion resistance, increasing the polarization resistance from 255 Ω.cm2 to 2756 Ω.cm2 and reducing the corrosion rate by more than one order of magnitude. In addition, tribological tests conducted in SBF demonstrated a stable and low coefficient of friction (∼0.15), indicating improved surface integrity under wet sliding conditions. A comparative analysis revealed that Ar+ etching is more effective than mechanical polishing in improving the structure, morphology, and corrosion resistance of the Q-Dia coatings. These results demonstrate that Q-Dia films act as an effective protective barrier against corrosive degradation, highlighting their strong potential as surface coatings for biodegradable Mg-based implant applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of (a) the extrusion process used to fabricate the Mg–Ca alloy and the dimensions of the disc-shaped specimens, and (b) the surface pretreatment procedures applied to the Mg–Ca alloy prior to coating, including mechanical polishing and in situ Ar+ etching.
Schematic of the coaxial arc plasma deposition (CAPD) method, representative plasma pulses during deposition, and Q-Dia films on Mg–Ca alloy samples prepared with different surface pretreatments.

Schematic diagrams of (a) the three-electrode electrochemical cell for corrosion testing in SBF and (b) the tribological test configuration using an Al2O3 ball sliding against bare and Q-Dia-coated Mg–Ca alloy surfaces under SBF lubrication.

Top-view SEM images of bare Mg–Ca alloy under (a) mechanical polishing treatment and (b) Ar + etching treatment. Q-Dia coatings deposited on Mg–Ca alloy: (c, e) Q-Dia1 coating with surface pretreatment by polishing, and (d, f) Q-Dia2 coating with surface pretreatment by Ar etching.
a) Cross-sectional SEM images and b) cross-sectional EDS scan of Q-Dia film on Mg–Ca alloy. Surface EDS spectra of c) bare Mg–Ca alloy and d) Q-Dia film on Mg–Ca alloy.
Surface roughness of bare Mg–Ca substrate under a) mechanical polishing and b) Ar+ etching treatment measured by 3D laser confocal microscopy.

AFM surface morphologies and corresponding three-dimensional topographic images of quench-produced diamond coatings deposited on Mg–Ca alloys: (a) Q-Dia1 coating prepared on mechanically polished substrate and (b) Q-Dia2 coating prepared on Ar-ion-etched substrate.
X-ray photoelectron spectra of a) bare Mg–Ca alloy and b) Q-Dia film. c) High-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum and d) Raman spectrum of the Q-Dia film.
Open circuit potential evolution of bare Mg–Ca alloy, Q-Dia1 and Q-Dia2 immersed in a SBF solution.
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy results in SBF solution: (a) Nyquist plots; (b, c) Bode plots of the bare Mg–Ca alloy, Q-Dia1, and Q-Dia2 films.
Equivalent circuit models used for fitting the EIS data of a) the bare Mg–Ca alloy and b) Q-Dia films.
Polarization resistance of the bare Mg–Ca alloy, Q-Dia1, and Q-Dia2 films.
Potentiodynamic polarization results in SBF solution: (a) polarization curves and (b) corrosion rate of the bare Mg–Ca alloy, Q-Dia1, and Q-Dia2 films.
A schematic of the corrosion and protection mechanism in SBF solution for (a–c) Q-Dia1 and (d–f) Q-Dia2 coatings on Mg–Ca alloy.

Scratch test results of the Q-Dia2 coating on the Mg–Ca alloy: (a) Optical image of the scratch track with tangential force as a function of scratch length and the corresponding critical loads (LC2 and LC3); (b) normal force, acoustic emission, and penetration depth signals recorded during the scratch test.

Tribological behavior under wet sliding conditions in SBF: (a) friction curves; (b) average coefficient of friction (COF) and wear rate of the of the Al2O3 counter-body; (c, d) wear track morphologies of the bare Mg–Ca alloy and the Q-Dia2-coated sample tested against an Al2O3 counter-body under normal loads of 2 N and 4 N for 10 min.
Experimental parameters used for Ar+ etching and Q-Dia deposition by CAPD.
Electrochemical parameters obtained from EIS fit for the bare Mg–Ca alloy and Q-Dia film in SBF solution.

Electrochemical parameters (E corr and i
corr ) determined from potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) measurements.

head.ce:title: Improved corrosion resistance and surface stability of Mg–Ca alloys in simulated body fluid by quench-produced diamond coatings

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Magnesium alloys are attractive biodegradable materials for biomedical implants; however, their clinical application is severely limited by rapid corrosion in physiological environments. In this study, Quench-produced Diamond (Q-Dia) films were deposited on Mg–Ca alloy substrates at room temperature using the coaxial arc plasma deposition technique. Two surface pretreatments were employed prior to coating: (i) mechanical polishing and (ii) in-situ argon ion (Ar+) etching, to clarify their influence on film formation and corrosion behavior. The surface morphology and bonding structure of the Q-Dia films were characterized by scanning electron microscopy, atomic force microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and Raman spectroscopy, revealing dense diamond-like grain growth with mixed sp2/sp3 carbon bonding and a pronounced D-band feature. Corrosion performance was evaluated in simulated body fluid (SBF) at 37 °C using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization (PDP), with bare Mg–Ca alloy as a reference. The Q-Dia coating significantly enhanced corrosion resistance, increasing the polarization resistance from 255 Ω.cm2 to 2756 Ω.cm2 and reducing the corrosion rate by more than one order of magnitude. In addition, tribological tests conducted in SBF demonstrated a stable and low coefficient of friction (∼0.15), indicating improved surface integrity under wet sliding conditions. A comparative analysis revealed that Ar+ etching is more effective than mechanical polishing in improving the structure, morphology, and corrosion resistance of the Q-Dia coatings. These results demonstrate that Q-Dia films act as an effective protective barrier against corrosive degradation, highlighting their strong potential as surface coatings for biodegradable Mg-based implant applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Biodegradable metals such as magnesium (Mg), iron (Fe), and zinc (Zn) have attracted increasing interest as alternatives to conventional permanent implant materials, owing to their potential to eliminate secondary removal surgeries and reduce long-term complications associated with non-degradable metallic implants [1]. Among these materials, Mg-based alloys are particularly attractive due to their low density, elastic modulus close to that of natural bone, and favorable biocompatibility. To meet mechanical and functional requirements, Mg alloys are commonly alloyed with elements such as Al, Zn, and Ca, and their compositions are carefully tailored to balance mechanical performance, corrosion behavior, and biological compatibility [2].

In particular, Mg–Ca alloys have received special attention because calcium is an essential biological element and can improve both biocompatibility and mechanical properties through grain refinement and precipitation strengthening [2]. Nevertheless, the clinical translation of Mg-based implants remains limited by their high corrosion rate in body fluids, which leads to rapid hydrogen evolution, pH rise, and mechanical instability before sufficient tissue healing. Consequently, extensive efforts have been devoted to developing surface modification strategies-including conversion treatments, electrochemical processes, and protective coatings-to enhance the corrosion resistance of Mg alloys without compromising their biocompatibility [3,4].

Among protective coatings, diamond-based materials have emerged as promising candidates due to their outstanding hardness, chemical inertness, low friction, and proven biocompatibility [5,6]. However, direct deposition of high-quality diamond coatings on metallic substrates remains challenging. For metals such as Fe, Co, and Ni, surface-mediated catalytic effects during deposition favor the formation of sp2-bonded carbon, thereby suppressing diamond nucleation and growth [7]. Moreover, refractory metals including Zr, Ti, Hf, Nb, and Ta typically exhibit low diamond nucleation densities, and the resulting films often suffer from poor continuity due to lattice mismatch and high interfacial stresses between metal carbide layers and diamond phases [8]. For Mg alloys in particular, the highly reactive surface and rapid formation of native oxide layers significantly hinder diamond nucleation and promote sp2-rich carbon formation during deposition, making interlayer-free diamond growth especially difficult. As a result, the application of diamond coatings on Mg-based alloys has remained limited [9].

To circumvent these challenges, many studies have focused on incorporating nanodiamond (ND) particles directly into Mg alloy matrices. Previous reports have shown that ND additions can significantly influence the microstructure, mechanical properties, and corrosion behavior of Mg-based alloys [10,11]. For instance, Gong et al. demonstrated that uniform dispersion of 5 wt% ND particles in a Mg matrix reduced the corrosion rate by approximately 4.5 times, which was attributed to chemical interactions and physical adsorption of ions from simulated body fluid onto ND surfaces [12]. Other studies on ND-reinforced Mg-Zn-Ca alloys reported pronounced grain refinement accompanied by increases in nanohardness and elastic modulus with increasing ND content [13].

Despite these improvements, ND-reinforced Mg composites differ fundamentally from surface-engineered coatings. While bulk reinforcement can enhance mechanical properties and partially influence corrosion behavior, it does not provide a continuous barrier against electrolyte penetration or effectively suppress localized corrosion initiated at surface defects and second-phase particles. In contrast, a dense and conformal diamond-based coating can function as an effective physical and chemical barrier, directly isolating the Mg substrate from aggressive environments.

To improve adhesion and coating stability, most diamond or diamond-like carbon coating studies on Mg alloys have employed metallic or ceramic interlayers (e.g., Ti, Cr, TiC, or multilayer architectures). Although such interlayers can enhance adhesion and corrosion resistance, they increase process complexity and may introduce additional galvanic corrosion risks, particularly in aqueous or physiological environments [14,15].

An alternative and less explored approach is to engineer the substrate surface state prior to coating deposition. Surface pretreatments such as mechanical polishing and plasma-based treatments can substantially modify surface roughness, oxide thickness, defect density, and surface energy, all of which critically influence carbon nucleation behavior and coating adhesion. In our previous studies [16–18], in situ Ar+ etching was shown to effectively remove native oxide layers from titanium substrates and promote the growth of quench-produced diamond (Q-Dia) coatings, resulting in thick (∼3 μm), dense films with high hardness (∼54 GPa) and promising tribological performance. However, systematic investigations of surface pretreatment–assisted, interlayer-free diamond coating growth on Mg–Ca alloys remain scarce. Moreover, the corrosion and tribological performance of such coatings under simulated service environments has not been evaluated.

In the present work, quench-produced diamond (Q-Dia) coatings were directly deposited on Mg–1wt.%Ca alloys following two distinct surface pretreatments: Ar+ etching and mechanical polishing, without employing any interlayer. The surface morphology, chemical bonding, and structural characteristics of the Q-Dia coatings were systematically analyzed using SEM, Raman spectroscopy, and XPS. The corrosion behavior was evaluated in simulated body fluid (SBF) through electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and potentiodynamic polarization (PDP). Furthermore, the tribological performance was investigated under SBF-lubricated conditions to assess the combined effects of corrosion and wear, providing insight into the feasibility of interlayer-free diamond coatings for Mg–Ca alloy surface protection.

The magnesium–calcium alloy used in this study was fabricated by Amli Materials Technology Co., Ltd. (Taoyuan, Taiwan) via conventional smelting, with a nominal composition of 1 wt% calcium and 99 wt% magnesium. The as-cast ingot was subsequently extruded by Advanced Material Specialty Inc. into cylindrical rods of 25.4 mm diameter. The extruded rods were cut into disc-shaped specimens with a thickness of 2.0 mm, resulting in final sample dimensions of 25 mm × 2 mm. The samples were then carefully polished to obtain a smooth, mirror-like reflective surface finish, as shown in Fig. 1a. The specimens were then ultrasonically cleaned in acetone to remove residual debris, rinsed thoroughly, and dried under a nitrogen stream.

Despite careful surface preparation, exposure to ambient air after polishing resulted in the spontaneous formation of a thin MgO surface layer. In certain deposition processes, such as atomic layer deposition (ALD), this naturally formed oxide has been reported to facilitate the nucleation and growth of oxide coatings, including ZrO2 films [19]. In contrast, for quench-produced diamond (Q-Dia) deposition, the presence of the native oxide layer was found to hinder coating adhesion(Fig. S1 and S2, Supplementary Materials), leading to weakened interfacial bonding and compromised coating integrity [20]. Consequently, additional surface pretreatment steps were employed prior to Q-Dia deposition, as described in Section 2.2.

Prior to Q-Dia films deposition, two surface pretreatment methods were applied to minimize or remove the naturally formed oxide layer on the Mg–Ca alloy surface. In the first pretreatment, the Mg–Ca alloy specimens were sequentially polished using SiC abrasive papers with grit sizes of P800, P1500, and P4000 (Fig. 1b). The polished samples were subsequently ultrasonically cleaned in acetone for 5 min at a sonication power of 80 W and a frequency of 38 kHz to remove residual particulates and organic contaminants. After cleaning, the specimens were dried under an argon gas stream and immediately transferred into the deposition chamber to minimize surface reoxidation. Samples prepared using this pretreatment were denoted as Q-Dia1.

For the second pretreatment, the Mg–Ca alloy specimens were first ultrasonically cleaned in acetone for 5 min and dried under an argon gas stream before being introduced into the deposition chamber. In situ Ar+ ion etching was then performed using an ion gun to remove the native MgO layer and residual surface contaminants. The etching was carried out under an Ar gas flow rate of 4.3 sccm, an ion gun operating voltage of 1 kV, and a chamber pressure below 8 × 10−2 Pa. A DC substrate bias of −300 V was applied to accelerate Ar+ ions toward the Mg–Ca surface, with the etching duration set to 3 min. Following etching, the substrate holder was rotated from the ion gun position to face the arc source, enabling immediate Q-Dia deposition without air exposure. Samples treated by this method were designated as Q-Dia2.

Quench-produced Diamond (Q-Dia) films were deposited onto Mg–Ca alloy substrates using the coaxial arc plasma deposition (CAPD) system equipped with an arc plasma gun (ULVAC, APG-1000). A high-purity graphite rod (φ 10 × 30 mm, 99.99%, Toshima, Japan) was employed as the carbon source and positioned approximately 20 mm from the substrate surface. The arc plasma gun was connected to a 720 μF capacitor and operated at an applied voltage of 100 V. Previously, the CAPD technique, using a different chamber configuration, has been employed for the growth of nanodiamond films on Si substrates at elevated temperatures (∼550 °C) with flowing nitrogen gas to produce nitrogen-doped nanodiamond films [21,22]. In contrast, the present work employed CAPD at room temperature without any reactive gas flow. The system was operated at a repetition rate of 1 Hz, and the chamber was evacuated to a base pressure of approximately 1 × 10−5 Pa prior to deposition. Film deposition was carried out for approximately 80 min. A schematic illustration of the CAPD system, along with the growth mechanism, is presented in Fig. 2 , and the detailed operational parameters are summarized in Table 1 . The resulting Q-Dia/Mg–Ca samples prepared with different pretreatments are also shown in Fig. 2.

Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JEOL JSM-IT700HR) was employed to examine the surface morphology and microstructure features of Q-Dia films. Top-view SEM images provided information on surface features, while cross-sectional SEM images were used to determine film thickness. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was used for both survey and localized analyses to evaluate the elemental composition of the Q-Dia films. Atomic force microscopy (AFM; Bruker Dimension ICON, Santa Barbara, CA, USA) was used to characterize the surface morphology at the nanoscale, and the acquired data were analyzed using Nanoscope Analysis 3 software.

Raman spectroscopy, a non-destructive technique well suited for distinguishing carbon allotropes, was utilized to analyze the bonding structure of Q-Dia films. Microscopic visible Raman measurements were performed using a MicroRam-ARAMIS (HORIBA Ltd.) equipped with a 532 nm excitation laser and a confocal microscope. A 5% laser filter was applied to reduce laser intensity and avoid noise or thermally induced graphitization. The Raman wavenumber axis was calibrated using a standard single-crystal silicon reference with a characteristic peak at 520 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Thermo K-Alpha, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) was employed to investigate the surface chemical composition and bonding states of the Q-Dia films. Survey spectra were collected using Al Kα radiation with a photon energy of 1486.6 eV, while high-resolution C 1s spectra were acquired at a photon energy of 350 eV. The relative contributions of sp2- and sp3-bonded carbon were determined by fitting the Raman and XPS spectra using Gaussian functions with OriginPro software (OriginLab Corporation, USA).

The adhesion of the Q-Dia films was evaluated by scratch testing using a Rockwell AY-242 indenter with a tip radius of 200 μm. The tests were performed at a loading rate of 100 N/min up to a maximum load of 40 N, with a scratching speed of 12.56 mm/min and a scratch length of 4.90 mm. The critical load was determined by optical microscopy examination of the resulting scratch tracks.

Electrochemical tests were conducted to evaluate the corrosion resistance of the bare Mg–Ca alloy and Q-Dia films in simulated body fluid (SBF) at room temperature. The SBF solution was prepared to simulate physiological conditions and consisted of 6.54 g/L NaCl, 0.37 g/L KCl, 2.26 g/L NaHCO3, 0.07 g/L Na2SO4, 0.17 g/L Na2HPO4·2H2O, 0.36 g/L CaCl2·2H2O, 0.31 g/L MgCl2·6H2O, and 6.1 g/L tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris). Tris was added slowly while continuously monitoring the pH. After each addition, sufficient time was allowed for complete dissolution and stabilization of the pH before further adjustment. This process was repeated until the final pH was adjusted to 7.40 ± 0.05, in accordance with established SBF preparation protocols [23].

Electrochemical measurements were performed using a custom-designed three-electrode cell, with the specimen serving as the working electrode (exposed area of 1.767 cm2), a platinum sheet as the counter electrode, and a saturated calomel electrode (Ag/AgCl) as the reference electrode, as shown in Fig. 3a. The experiments, including electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization (PDP), were carried out using a potentiostat (VersaSTAT 3F, AMETEK Inc., USA). Prior to testing, the system was stabilized until the open-circuit potential (OCP) drift was less than 10 mV over 2000 s. In the EIS measurements, a small sinusoidal perturbation of ±10 mV around the open-circuit potential (OCP) was applied over a frequency range from 105 to 10−2 Hz, and impedance values were normalized to the exposed surface area and reported in Ω.cm2. Equivalent circuit fitting was performed using ZSimpWin software. PDP measurements were conducted following the EIS tests using a scan rate of 0.5 mV/s over a potential range from −0.4 V to +1.2 V relative to the OCP. Considering the complex corrosion behavior of magnesium-based systems, the corrosion current density (icorr) was estimated by Tafel extrapolation applied to the cathodic branch within ±120 mV of Ecorr, using Nova 2.1 software.

Tribological tests were performed at room temperature under simulated body fluid (SBF) lubrication, which served as the primary testing environment to replicate physiologically relevant conditions. The experiments were carried out using a TRB3 tribometer with an alumina (Al2O3) ball with a diameter of 6 mm as the counter-body against both the bare Mg–Ca alloy substrate and the Q-Dia films (Fig. 3b). Sliding tests were conducted at a constant velocity of 5 cm/s under two different loads of 2 N and 4 N for a duration of 600 s. The wear resistance was evaluated by measuring the wear track depth using three-dimensional laser confocal microscopy (Olympus LEXT OLS 5100-EAT).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterization of Q-Dia coatings
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 presents the SEM surface morphologies of bare Mg–Ca alloys subjected to different pretreatment methods, as well as Q-Dia coatings deposited on Mg–Ca substrates after these treatments. Fig. 4a and b illustrates the surface conditions after pretreatment: the mechanically polished sample shows clearly visible polishing grooves, whereas the Ar+-etched surface exhibits localized regions where the native oxide layer has been removed, resulting in a cleaner and more activated alloy surface. In Fig. 4c–f, the coatings exhibit a uniform and compact surface morphology with continuous coverage over the substrate. No visible cracks, pinholes, or delamination are observed at the micrometer scale, indicating good coating integrity. The homogeneous surface features suggest stable nucleation and growth of the Q-Dia films across the Mg–Ca alloy. Moreover, the successful deposition of Q-Dia films without an interlayer indicates that the applied surface pretreatments effectively suppressed the influence of the native oxide layer and promoted sufficient interfacial adhesion between the coating and the substrate.

For the Q-Dia1 film deposited on the polished Mg–Ca alloy, surface grooves originating from the polishing process remain clearly visible even at high magnification (Fig. 4c and e). These polishing-induced grooves appear to facilitate Q-Dia nucleation and contribute to improved interfacial bonding between the coating and the substrate. High-magnification SEM images reveal a densely packed granular morphology characteristic of Q-Dia films. The coating exhibits a typical cauliflower-like structure composed of nearly spherical domes with relatively uniform size, indicating a compact and dense film structure [25]. This morphology suggests effective clustering and uniform growth behavior during deposition. The columnar growth features observed in the coating are attributed to the highly energetic carbon ions (C+) generated by the CAPD process, which dominate the film growth mechanism [52]. In addition, a noticeable difference in growth uniformity is observed between the Q-Dia1 and Q-Dia2 films (Fig. 4d and f), which can be attributed to the different surface pretreatment methods applied to the Mg–Ca alloy, significantly influencing the subsequent Q-Dia film growth.

The cross-sectional SEM image in Fig. 5a shows the Q-Dia film deposited on the Mg–Ca alloy with a thickness of approximately 2.4 μm. The corresponding EDS line scan across the coating cross section (Fig. 5b) illustrates a sharp transition in elemental distribution, with a carbon-rich region (red) corresponding to the Q-Dia film and Mg signal (blue) originating from the substrate. As shown in Fig. 5c, the EDS spectrum of the bare Mg–Ca alloy exhibits a predominant Mg peak accompanied by relatively lower Ca and O peaks, which are attributed to the alloy composition and the presence of a native oxide layer on the surface. In contrast, the surface EDS spectrum of the Q-Dia film in Fig. 5d is dominated by a carbon (C) signal, with no detectable Mg or Ca peaks, indicating complete surface coverage by the Q-Dia coating.

Fig. 6 compares the 3D surface topography and roughness profiles of Mg–Ca alloy surfaces subjected to two different pretreatments: mechanical polishing and Ar+ ion etching. The 3D maps of the mechanically polished surface (Fig. 6a) reveal pronounced linear grooves generated during polishing. These grooves produce directional surface features and significant height variations, which are reflected in the roughness profile by noticeable fluctuations along the measurement length. The average surface roughness of this sample is approximately 1112 nm, which can negatively influence coating uniformity and interfacial adhesion.

In contrast, the Ar+-etched surface exhibits a much more homogeneous and isotropic topography. The ion-etching process removes the deformed surface layer and effectively reduces the native oxide film, resulting in a cleaner and more activated substrate. The average roughness decreases to about 270 nm, and the corresponding roughness profile appears smoother and more stable, as illustrated in Fig. 6b. These results demonstrate that Ar+ etching produces a more uniform for Mg–Ca surface, which is expected to promote coating nucleation, enhance interfacial bonding, and ultimately improve the adhesion of the Q-Dia film compared with the mechanically polished substrate.

Fig. 7 presents the AFM surface morphologies and corresponding three-dimensional topographic images of the Q-Dia film surfaces, together with their average surface roughness (Ra) values. For the Q-Dia1 coating deposited on the mechanically polished substrate, pronounced polishing-induced grooves are clearly visible, indicating that the initial surface condition strongly affects the resulting film morphology. As a consequence, the Q-Dia1 film exhibits a relatively high surface roughness (Ra = 100 nm), as shown in Fig. 7a. In contrast, the Q-Dia2 coating deposited on the Ar-ion-etched substrate shows a much smoother surface with a significantly reduced roughness (Ra = 32 nm), as demonstrated in Fig. 7b. This reduction in surface roughness is attributed to the effective removal of surface irregularities and contaminants by Ar etching, which suppresses defect formation and promotes film densification [27]. A smoother and more homogeneous substrate surface facilitates more uniform nucleation and growth during deposition, resulting in a compact microstructure with fewer voids and surface defects [28]. Such enhanced densification is expected to improve the structural stability and protective performance of the Q-Dia coating. These AFM observations are fully consistent with the SEM surface morphologies shown in Fig. 4.

X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) is a powerful surface-sensitive technique that enables precise analysis of the chemical structure of thin film surfaces by detecting photo-generated electrons from core-level states, such as the C 1s level. Fig. 8a–c show the XPS results of the bare Mg–Ca alloy and the Q-Dia film. The survey spectrum of the bare Mg–Ca alloy (Fig. 8a) exhibits distinct Mg 2p, Ca 2p, O 1s, and C 1s peaks, indicating the presence of native Mg- and Ca-based oxide layers, together with a minor contribution from adventitious carbon due to atmospheric exposure [29]. In contrast, the survey spectrum of the Q-Dia film (Fig. 8b) is dominated by a strong C 1s peak, with no detectable Mg or Ca signals, indicating complete surface coverage by the Q-Dia film. Additional XPS measurements were performed for both Q-Dia1 and Q-Dia2 samples, as presented in Fig. S3 of the Supplementary Materials. The spectra show no significant differences between the two coatings, confirming that both films provide uniform and continuous surface coverage.

The high-resolution C 1s spectrum of the Q-Dia film (Fig. 8c) was deconvoluted into sp2- and sp3-bonded carbon components, along with minor oxygen-containing functional groups (C–O, C–O–C, and COOH) [30]. The peak at 284.42 eV is attributed to sp2-bonded carbon (C=C), while the peak centered at 285.2 eV corresponds to sp3-bonded carbon (C–C), characteristic configuration of the diamond phase. Quantitative fitting yields a sp3 carbon fraction of approximately 58% and a sp2 fraction of about 42%, indicating a diamond-rich carbon matrix with an amorphous carbon component.

Complementary to the XPS analysis, Raman spectroscopy was employed to further investigate the bonding configuration and structural characteristics of the Q-Dia film. As shown in Fig. 8d, visible Raman spectroscopy using a 532 nm excitation laser was performed on the Q-Dia film deposited on the Mg–Ca alloy. The Raman spectrum exhibits three characteristic peaks at approximately 1353, 1498, and 1578 cm−1, corresponding to the D band, trans-polyacetylene (t-PA) band, and G band, respectively [31]. In Q-Dia films, the D band associated with disordered sp2-bonded carbon is known to overlap with the diamond characteristic peak at 1332 cm−1 under visible excitation [32]. Owing to the high density of grain boundaries in Q-Dia films, Raman scattering is more sensitive to sp2-bonded carbon with a narrower band gap than to sp3-bonded carbon in nanodiamond grains [33,34].

The peak at 1498 cm−1 is attributed to t-PA chains located at grain boundaries and is commonly regarded as a fingerprint of nanodiamond-containing carbon films [20]. In addition, the prominent G band at 1578 cm−1 indicates sp2-bonded carbon in a constrained environment, consistent with the presence of sp3-rich chains embedded within a dense tetrahedral amorphous carbon matrix in quench-produced diamond films. The G band typically appears in the range of 1510–1580 cm−1 and may shift toward higher wavenumbers under increased bond constraint [16]. Deconvolution of the G band reveals three components located at approximately 1556 cm−1 (G1), 1562 cm−1 (G2), and 1590 cm−1 (G3). The G1 and G2 components are associated with sp2-bonded carbon arranged in ring and chain configurations, while the higher-wavenumber G3 component reflects increased bond disorder and local structural constraint within the sp2 network.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Corrosion behavior of Q-Dia-coated Mg–Ca alloys

ce:section.ce:para: Gao et al. [35] and Sun et al. [36] have reported that the open circuit potential (OCP) can serve as a reliable indicator of a material's resistance to corrosion in aqueous media. In general, coatings that exhibit more positive OCP values are associated with improved corrosion resistance and reduced susceptibility to degradation. Fig. 9 presents the time-dependent evolution of the open-circuit potential for the bare Mg–Ca alloy, Q-Dia1, and Q-Dia2 samples during immersion in simulated body fluid (SBF) solution. The observed fluctuations in the OCP pattern may be attributed to the formation of corrosion products that induce local passivation of the coating surface, thereby shifting the dominant corrosion activity to alternative sites [37].

The bare Mg–Ca alloy exhibits a lower OCP value (average of −1.87 V), indicating a higher susceptibility to corrosion. In contrast, the Q-Dia1 and Q-Dia2 coatings show more positive OCP values of −1.54 V and − 1.52 V, respectively, suggesting improved corrosion resistance compared to the uncoated alloy. Moreover, the OCP curve of Q-Dia2 displays lower fluctuations and a slightly more stable potential than that of Q-Dia1, indicating that the Ar+ ion etching pretreatment is an effective approach for enhancing the corrosion protection of the Mg–Ca alloy by the Q-Dia coating.

The EIS plots of the bare Mg–Ca alloy and Q-Dia film immersed in SBF solution are shown in Fig. 10 . The diameter of the semicircle in the Nyquist plot is commonly associated with the charge transfer resistance at the electrode-electrolyte interface [38]. As noted by Meng et al., interpretation of EIS data in SBF environments is intrinsically challenging because the electrode surface continuously evolves during immersion [39]. The high-frequency domain primarily represents the solution resistance and rapid interfacial electrochemical responses, whereas the intermediate-frequency range is governed by the properties of the coating and surface film. In contrast, the low-frequency region reflects corrosion kinetics, mass-transport processes, and the long-term evolution of the electrode surface [40,41]. As shown in Fig. 10a, the bare Mg–Ca alloy exhibits a small semicircular arc, indicating low impedance. In comparison, the semicircular loop of the Q-Dia1 film is approximately five times larger than that of the bare alloy, while the Q-Dia2 film exhibits a further enlarged semicircular arc with an impedance about eight times higher than that of the bare Mg–Ca alloy. These results indicate an increased charge transfer resistance for the Q-Dia film, with the Q-Dia2 film providing the highest corrosion resistance in SBF solution.

The Bode plots (Fig. 10b and c) show the variations in impedance magnitude and phase angle as a function of frequency. At high frequencies, all samples exhibit similar impedance values, indicating comparable corrosion behavior. The dispersion observed in the low-frequency range (≈10−2–10−1 Hz) is primarily associated with the inherently non-steady corrosion behavior of Mg alloys in SBF, particularly during the extended measurement time required at low frequencies. In this regime, the impedance response is governed by time-dependent interfacial phenomena, including the ongoing formation and dissolution of corrosion products such as Mg(OH)2 and Ca–P compounds, localized film rupture followed by re-passivation, and hydrogen evolution accompanied by intermittent bubble accumulation [42,43]. These processes dynamically alter the effective electrochemically active area and induce fluctuations in the electrochemical response, which manifest as scattered data points in the low-frequency region [44]. Despite this dispersion, the Q-Dia2 coating consistently maintains a substantially higher impedance magnitude than both Q-Dia1 and the bare Mg–Ca alloy across the measured frequency range, demonstrating its enhanced barrier effectiveness. The phase angle also increases from −27.9° for the bare Mg–Ca alloy to −32.8° for Q-Dia1 and − 50° for Q-Dia2 (Fig. 10c). The higher phase angle of the Q-Dia2 film indicates more pronounced capacitive behavior, which is associated with improved barrier properties and corrosion protection [45,46].

The electrochemical impedance behavior of the bare Mg–Ca alloy and the Q-Dia films immersed in SBF solution is described using the equivalent circuit shown in Fig. 11 a and b. The model accounts for contributions from the electrolyte, coating layer, interfacial processes, and inductive effects. The solution resistance of the SBF electrolyte is represented by
R s
. The non-ideal capacitive behavior of the Q-Dia film is described by the constant phase element
Q f
with an exponent
n f
, while the corresponding film resistance is denoted by
R f
. The interfacial double-layer capacitance at the electrode/coating or corrosion product interface is represented by
Q dl
, and the charge transfer resistance associated with interfacial electrochemical reactions is denoted by
R ct
. The low-frequency inductive response is modeled using an inductance L
and a series resistance
R L
, which are commonly associated with adsorption-related or relaxation processes during corrosion [47,48].

The EIS fitting results are summarized in Table 2 , listing the parameters obtained from the equivalent circuit model described above. The quality of the fitting was evaluated using the chi-square (χ2) values, which remained below ∼10−2 for all samples, indicating good agreement between the experimental and simulated EIS spectra [49]. The EIS data were fitted with an average fitting error of 1.79% for the bare Mg–Ca alloy, 5.31% for Q-Dia1, and 8.93% for Q-Dia2, all remaining below 10%, which indicates satisfactory fitting quality. The relatively higher fitting error for Q-Dia1 and Q-Dia2 is attributed to increased data dispersion in the low-frequency region, likely associated with coating heterogeneity and diffusion-related processes. For the bare Mg–Ca alloy, the film resistance (
R f
) and the associated constant phase element (
Q f
) in the two-time-constant equivalent circuit are attributed to the naturally formed surface oxide layer on the alloy. Electrochemically, this native film behaves as a thin interfacial barrier that partially impedes charge transfer and contributes to the measured impedance response. Within this framework,
R f
represents the resistive contribution of the oxide layer, whereas
Q f
describes its non-ideal capacitive behavior, which is governed by factors such as film thickness, porosity, and surface heterogeneity. The dispersion exponent
n f
reflects the deviation from ideal capacitive behavior, with values of 0.78 and 0.83 for the Q-Dia1 and Q-Dia2 films, respectively. The higher
n f
value of the Q-Dia2 film indicates more pronounced capacitive behavior, suggesting a more homogeneous coating structure and protective coating layer [50]. The film resistance
R f
decreases from 229.6 Ω.cm2 for the bare Mg–Ca alloy to 109.2 and 54.1 Ω.cm2 for the Q-Dia1 and Q-Dia2 films, respectively, which may be related to electrolyte penetration through the outer Q-Dia layer. In contrast, the charge transfer resistance
R ct
increases markedly from 26.39 Ω.cm2 for the bare alloy to 2041 and 2702 Ω.cm2 for the Q-Dia1 and Q-Dia2 coatings, respectively. The higher
R ct
values indicate suppressed charge transfer kinetics, with the Q-Dia2 coating exhibiting the highest corrosion resistance.

Corrosion resistance was further evaluated using the polarization resistance (
R p
). The polarization resistance was estimated from the sum of the film resistance and charge-transfer resistance (
R f
+
R ct
). Higher
R p
values correspond to lower anodic and cathodic reaction rates and thus enhanced corrosion resistance [51]. As shown in Fig. 12 , the bare Mg–Ca alloy exhibits an
R p
value of 256 Ω.cm2, which increases to 2150 and 2756 Ω.cm2 for the Q-Dia1 and Q-Dia2 coatings, respectively. Notably, both Q-Dia1 and Q-Dia2 demonstrate higher
R p
values than the Q-Dia/TiC/Mg–Ca alloy reported in our previous work (1771.8 Ω.cm2) [52]. This improvement clearly indicates that appropriate surface pretreatment prior to coating deposition plays a critical role in enhancing corrosion resistance, even in the absence of an interlayer. The significantly higher
R p
values of the Q-Dia film indicate improved corrosion resistance compared with the bare alloy. The difference in
R p
between Q-Dia1 and Q-Dia2, approximately 600 Ω.cm2, is associated with differences in surface pretreatment and coating characteristics.

Fig. 13a shows the PDP curves of the bare Mg–Ca alloy and the Q-Dia1 and Q-Dia2 film in SBF solution. The corrosion current densities (
i corr
) were determined by Tafel extrapolation and are summarized in Table 3 . The bare Mg–Ca alloy exhibits an
i corr
of 1.02 ×
10
− 4
A.cm−2. After Q-Dia deposition, the
i corr
values decrease to 2.58 ×
10
− 5
A.cm−2 for Q-Dia1 and 7.33 ×
10
− 6
A.cm−2 for Q-Dia2. Compared with the bare Mg–Ca alloy, the corrosion current density is reduced by approximately 4 times for Q-Dia1 and 14 times for Q-Dia2. In addition, the Q-Dia2 coating exhibits an approximately 3.5-fold lower
i corr
than Q-Dia1, which is associated with differences in surface pretreatment and coating characteristics. The coated samples also show a positive shift in corrosion potential relative to the bare Mg–Ca alloy, consistent with the EIS results and the mixed potential theory [27,53].

Fig. 13b presents the corrosion rates of the bare Mg–Ca alloy and the Q-Dia films. The corrosion rate was calculated using the following Eq. (1) [54–56]:
(1)

where CR is the corrosion rate (mm/year), EW is the equivalent weight of the metal (EW = M/n),
i corr
is the corrosion current density (A/cm2), and ρ is the density of the material (1.74 g/cm3). The molar mass (M) and valence (n) of magnesium are 24.3 g/mol and 2, respectively. The corrosion rates decrease from 2.33 ×
10

The Q-Dia1 and Q-Dia2 coatings exhibit protection efficiencies of 74.5% and 92.7%, respectively. Among the tested samples, the Q-Dia2 coating shows the lowest corrosion rate and highest protection efficiency, indicating superior corrosion protection.

The corrosion protection mechanisms differ between Q-Dia1 and Q-Dia2 due to the surface pretreatment conditions. For Q-Dia1, which was deposited on a mechanically polished substrate, corrosive ions could penetrate the coating through polishing grooves that extended close to the Mg–Ca surface, as illustrated in Fig. 14a–c. These defects locally weakened the coating integrity, leading to film breakdown, enhanced electrolyte access to the substrate, and accelerated Mg dissolution [58,59]. In addition, some surface regions retained native oxide residues or micro-defects that were not completely removed by polishing, further promoting localized corrosion initiation [60,61].

In contrast, Q-Dia2, which was deposited on an Ar-ion-etched substrate, exhibited improved corrosion resistance. The ion-etching process effectively removed the native oxide layer and surface contaminants, producing a cleaner and more active substrate that enhanced coating nucleation and adhesion. As a result, the Q-Dia2 coating formed a more compact and adherent barrier that limited electrolyte penetration, suppressed the interaction between corrosive ions and the Mg–Ca substrate, and consequently reduced Mg dissolution, as outlined in Fig. 14d–f. These findings are consistent with the surface roughness analysis as well as the electrochemical results obtained from EIS and PDP measurements, all of which confirm the superior corrosion protection performance of Q-Dia2. Therefore, Ar-ion etching plays a key role in promoting Q-Dia growth, improving coating adhesion, and enhancing corrosion protection.

The Q-Dia2 coating (prepared after Ar+ ion etching) exhibited superior corrosion performance compared with Q-Dia1 (prepared after mechanical polishing). Based on this improved performance, Q-Dia2 was selected as the optimized coating condition and, therefore, was used for subsequent scratch adhesion and tribological tests.

To evaluate the adhesion behavior of the Q-Dia2 coating on the Mg–Ca alloy, progressive load scratch tests were performed. Fig. 15 shows the scratch test results obtained under a continuously increasing normal load. Fig. 15a presents the optical image of the entire 5 mm scratch track, along with the tangential force evolution as a function of scratch length. Two critical loads are identified along the scratch track, with LC2 and LC3 values of 9.4 N and 12.2 N, respectively, corresponding to the onset of coating damage and subsequent coating removal. In contrast, Q-Dia1 showed inferior adhesion performance in the scratch test, as illustrated in Fig. S5 of the Supplementary Materials. The damage evolution along the scratch path is reflected by changes in the tangential force response. The measured adhesion strength values of the Q-Dia2 coating are higher than those reported for amorphous carbon coatings deposited on ZK60 magnesium alloy (4 N and 9 N) [62]. The corresponding normal force, acoustic emission, and penetration depth signals recorded during the scratch test are shown in Fig. 15b.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tribological behavior of Q-Dia coatings in simulated body fluid
ce:section.ce:para: The tribological performance of the Q-Dia2 coatings was evaluated under wet sliding conditions using a ball-on-disk tribometer, with an alumina (Al2O3) counter-body and simulated body fluid (SBF) as the lubricant to simulate biomedical environments. Fig. 16 summarizes the coefficient of friction (COF) behavior of the bare Mg–Ca alloy and the Q-Dia2-coated sample under applied normal loads of 2 N and 4 N. All tribological tests were conducted at a constant sliding speed and duration to isolate the effect of normal load on frictional behavior. The average COF values (Fig. 16a) of the bare Mg–Ca alloy at normal loads of 2 N and 4 N are approximately 0.30 and 0.35, respectively. This behavior is associated with direct contact between the Mg–Ca alloy surface and the alumina counter-body. In the presence of SBF, the formation and repeated disruption of a fragile surface layer contribute to the development of a pronounced wear track [63], resulting in a relatively stable COF with minor fluctuations over the 10 min sliding test.

For the Q-Dia2-coated sample, the average coefficients of friction at normal loads of 2 N and 4 N are approximately 0.16 and 0.14, respectively, as shown in Fig. 16b. During the initial ∼100 s of sliding, the COF is noticeably higher, reaching values of about 0.56 at 2 N and 0.54 at 4 N. After this initial stage, the COF decreases and stabilizes at lower values, indicating the establishment of a steady sliding regime. This frictional response differs markedly from the behavior of the bare Mg–Ca alloy, which exhibits higher and less stable COF values throughout the test.

The elevated COF observed during the initial sliding stage of the Q-Dia2 coating is associated with the interaction of surface asperities related to the cauliflower-like morphology typical of PVD-deposited carbon films. Transient spikes in the COF curves suggest the generation of wear debris during sliding [64]. With continued sliding, fragmentation of these asperities and debris contributes to a more stable contact condition, resulting in a reduced and steady COF. In contrast, the bare Mg–Ca alloy shows poor tribological performance under wet sliding conditions due to the combined effects of mechanical wear and corrosion in SBF, leading to repeated formation and rupture of a mechanically weak surface layer.

The lower counter-body wear rate at 4 N compared to 2 N is attributed to load-dependent tribological mechanisms, as summarized in Fig. 16b. Higher applied load (4 N) increases the real contact area and contact stress, accelerating tribo-induced structural transformations and promoting rapid formation of a stable solid lubricating tribolayer enriched in sp2-bonded carbon. This tribolayer smooths the contact interface, reduces adhesive interactions, and lowers both friction and counter-body wear. In contrast, at lower load (2 N), tribolayer formation is delayed, third-body debris persist, and adhesive wear dominates due to the significant hardness mismatch between the Q-Dia coating and the Al2O3 ball, leading to increased material transfer and higher counter-body wear. As sliding progresses under higher load, the dominant wear mechanism shifts from adhesive to mild abrasive wear, resulting in reduced wear and improved tribological stability [65].

Fig. 16c and d shows the wear tracks after tribological testing. Three-dimensional laser profilometry reveals localized surface features, which are attributed to the interaction between the coating and the SBF environment. Height profile analysis indicates that these features do not lead to extensive coating failure or significant material loss. Overall, the Q-Dia2 coating effectively suppresses severe wear compared with the bare Mg–Ca alloy under the tested tribo-corrosion conditions.

Mechanical polishing and Ar+ etching pretreatments were systematically investigated to clarify their effects on the adhesion, microstructure, and performance of quench-produced diamond (Q-Dia) coatings deposited on Mg–Ca alloys. While mechanical polishing enabled Q-Dia film formation, residual surface grooves and native oxide layers limited interfacial bonding and resulted in less uniform film growth. In contrast, Ar+ etching effectively removed surface oxides and contaminants, promoting strong coating–substrate adhesion and the formation of a compact, homogeneous nanograined Q-Dia microstructure, as confirmed by SEM observations. XPS and Raman analyses revealed a high sp3 carbon content with characteristic diamond-related bonding, contributing to enhanced mechanical integrity, as reflected by a high critical load of 9.4 N in scratch adhesion testing.

Electrochemical measurements demonstrated that the Q-Dia coating markedly improved the corrosion resistance of the Mg–Ca alloy in simulated body fluid, showing a reduced corrosion current density (
i corr
= 7.33 × 10−6 A.cm−2) and an increased polarization resistance (Rp = 2756 Ω.cm2). In addition, tribological evaluations under SBF lubrication revealed a low and stable coefficient of friction for the Q-Dia coating compared with the bare substrate. Overall, the results indicate that Ar-ion etching is an effective pretreatment method for achieving strong interfacial bonding and enhanced corrosion and tribological performance of Q-Dia-coated Mg–Ca alloys, offering a promising surface engineering strategy for improving the durability of magnesium-based materials in corrosive environments.
